insights.insight: The ad industry pays $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution that infers purchase intent. A cryptographically verified receipt eliminates the inference entirely — the conversion event is the signal, not a proxy for it.

insights.topic: Cookie deprecation creates a structural vacuum
insights.insight: Chrome's deprecation completes the collapse of the cross-site tracking primitive that most programmatic targeting was built on. First-party data fills the gap only within a single ecosystem — it is blind the moment a consumer leaves.

insights.topic: Platform silos leave cross-ecosystem purchase data unowned
insights.insight: Amazon, Visa, Google, and Meta each see a fragment of the purchase journey — none see the full transaction. The receipt layer sits below all four silos and is the only point where cross-ecosystem verified spend data can be aggregated without platform cooperation.

insights.topic: Physical commerce receipts entering the dataset
insights.insight: Home Depot and Starbucks receipts appearing in consecutive snapshots signals the dataset is moving beyond digital subscriptions into physical retail — the category CPG and brick-and-mortar advertisers need most and currently have the least verified data on.

insights.topic: Brand participation is not optional in the long run
insights.insight: Consumer-submitted receipts are valid whether a brand opts in or not, meaning brands are already being measured. The Nash equilibrium is participation — staying out does not prevent inclusion in the dataset, it only removes the ability to shape how that data is used.

A 13–17% single-period drop in GMV and receipt count at HB#92 was preceded by velocity/acceleration divergence across four prior snapshots — a leading indicator pattern. Recovery is underway but velocity remains compressed at 0.597x, meaning signal volume is not yet stable.
